Introduction to R-INLA and INLAjoint

Bayesian survival, longitudinal and joint models with INLA
VIBASS 9, Valencia, Spain

Denis Rustand

July 8 and 9, 2026

The R package INLA (R-INLA)

The R-INLA package implements the Integrated Nested Laplace Approximations methodology in R. It computes approximate posterior density functions for latent Gaussian models.

The interface is very general. It offers robust capabilities for advanced models but it requires specific knowledge and coding skills to specify complex structures.

Installation

You can install the package directly from the repository:

install.packages("INLA", 
                 repos=c(getOption("repos"),
                        INLA="https://inla.r-inla-download.org/R/stable"), dep=TRUE)

The inla() function

The main function to fit a model is inla(). It requires three main arguments: formula, data and family.

Fixed effects are added with +. Random effects are specified using the f() function.

Here is a conceptual example for a logistic regression with a random intercept:

library(INLA)
load("data_example.RData")
result_inla <- inla(y ~ x1 + x2 + f(ID, model = "iid"),
                    data = data_example, family = "binomial")

Extracting results

The fitted object contains posterior summaries and densities for all parameters.

summary(result_inla)
## Time used:
##     Pre = 0.281, Running = 0.195, Post = 0.0152, Total = 0.492 
## Fixed effects:
##               mean    sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant   mode kld
## (Intercept) -0.072 0.119     -0.307   -0.072      0.162 -0.072   0
## x1           1.096 0.096      0.909    1.095      1.286  1.095   0
## x2          -1.109 0.096     -1.297   -1.109     -0.922 -1.109   0
## 
## Random effects:
##   Name     Model
##     ID IID model
## 
## Model hyperparameters:
##                  mean    sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant mode
## Precision for ID 1.33 0.425      0.733     1.26       2.36 1.14
## 
## Marginal log-Likelihood:  -555.45 
##  is computed 
## Posterior summaries for the linear predictor and the fitted values are computed
## (Posterior marginals needs also 'control.compute=list(return.marginals.predictor=TRUE)')

You can extract the posterior summaries of the fixed effects:

result_inla$summary.fixed
##                    mean         sd 0.025quant    0.5quant 0.975quant
## (Intercept) -0.07225875 0.11918328 -0.3071696 -0.07210632  0.1617877
## x1           1.09584975 0.09614606  0.9086368  1.09538308  1.2857160
## x2          -1.10884990 0.09562705 -1.2972935 -1.10852679 -0.9222437
##                    mode          kld
## (Intercept) -0.07210709 1.948434e-08
## x1           1.09537978 6.055618e-09
## x2          -1.10852467 2.944466e-09

You can extract the exact marginal posterior densities (a data frame of x and y coordinates mapping the density):

result_inla$marginals.fixed
## $`(Intercept)`
##                  x            y
##  [1,] -0.609275144 0.0003564656
##  [2,] -0.535956600 0.0028779573
##  [3,] -0.451396692 0.0255161298
##  [4,] -0.352945551 0.2115710752
##  [5,] -0.307169586 0.4729424510
##  [6,] -0.268429915 0.8465781776
##  [7,] -0.224354032 1.4608067404
##  [8,] -0.194918192 1.9556909504
##  [9,] -0.171665689 2.3601804290
## [10,] -0.151798919 2.6886687890
## [11,] -0.134002318 2.9497461462
## [12,] -0.117561681 3.1484733985

You can plot these densities directly:

plot(result_inla$marginals.fixed$x1, type = "l")

For random effects or hyperparameters, you simply replace fixed with random, hyperpar, fitted.values or linear.predictor.

Finding documentation internally

INLA supports a massive number of likelihoods and latent models. You can explore them directly in the R console:

# List all available likelihood families
names(inla.models()$likelihood)
##   [1] "fl"                            "poisson"                      
##   [3] "npoisson"                      "nzpoisson"                    
##   [5] "xpoisson"                      "cenpoisson"                   
##   [7] "cenpoisson2"                   "gpoisson"                     
##   [9] "poisson.special1"              "0poisson"                     
##  [11] "0poissonS"                     "1poisson"                     
##  [13] "1poissonS"                     "bell"                         
##  [15] "0binomial"                     "0binomialS"                   
##  [17] "0nbinomial"                    "0nbinomialS"                  
##  [19] "binomialmix"                   "binomial"                     
##  [21] "xbinomial"                     "occupancy"                    
##  [23] "pom"                           "bgev"                         
##  [25] "gamma"                         "mgamma"                       
##  [27] "gammasv"                       "gammasurv"                    
##  [29] "mgammasurv"                    "gammajw"                      
##  [31] "gammajwsurv"                   "gammacount"                   
##  [33] "gammacountmean"                "qkumar"                       
##  [35] "qloglogistic"                  "qloglogisticsurv"             
##  [37] "beta"                          "obeta"                        
##  [39] "betabinomial"                  "betabinomialna"               
##  [41] "cbinomial"                     "nbinomial"                    
##  [43] "nbinomial2"                    "cennbinomial2"

##  [47] "stdgaussian"                   "gaussianjw"                   
##  [49] "agaussian"                     "ggaussian"                    
##  [51] "ggaussianS"                    "bcgaussian"                   
##  [53] "exppower"                      "sem"                          
##  [55] "rcpoisson"                     "tpoisson"                     
##  [57] "circularnormal"                "wrappedcauchy"                
##  [59] "iidgamma"                      "iidlogitbeta"                 
##  [61] "loggammafrailty"               "logistic"                     
##  [63] "sn"                            "gev"                          
##  [65] "lognormal"                     "lognormalsurv"                
##  [67] "exponential"                   "exponentialsurv"

##  [71] "weibullsurv"                   "loglogistic"

##  [75] "stochvolln"                    "stochvolsn"

##  [81] "zeroinflatedpoisson2"          "zeroinflatedcenpoisson0"      
##  [83] "zeroinflatedcenpoisson1"       "zeroinflatedbetabinomial0"    
##  [85] "zeroinflatedbetabinomial1"     "zeroinflatedbinomial0"        
##  [87] "zeroinflatedbinomial1"         "zeroinflatedbinomial2"        
##  [89] "zeroninflatedbinomial2"        "zeroninflatedbinomial3"       
##  [91] "zeroinflatedbetabinomial2"     "zeroinflatednbinomial0"       
##  [93] "zeroinflatednbinomial1"        "zeroinflatednbinomial1strata2"
##  [95] "zeroinflatednbinomial1strata3" "zeroinflatednbinomial2"       
##  [97] "t"                             "tstrata"                      
##  [99] "nmix"                          "nmixnb"                       
## [101] "gp"                            "egp"                          
## [103] "dgp"                           "logperiodogram"               
## [105] "tweedie"                       "fmri"                         
## [107] "fmrisurv"                      "gompertz"                     
## [109] "gompertzsurv"                  "dgompertzsurv"                
## [111] "vm"                            "nvm"                          
## [113] "lavm"                          "cloglike"
# Read documentation for a specific likelihood
inla.doc("zeroinflatedpoisson")

# List all available latent models for the f() function
names(inla.models()$latent)
##  [1] "linear"       "iid"          "mec"          "meb"          "rgeneric"    
##  [6] "cgeneric"     "rw1"          "rw2"          "crw2"         "prw2"        
## [11] "seasonal"     "besag"        "besag2"       "bym"          "bym2"        
## [16] "besagproper"  "besagproper2" "fgn"          "fgn2"         "ar1"         
## [21] "ar1c"         "ar"           "ou"           "intslope"     "generic"     
## [26] "generic0"     "generic1"     "generic2"     "generic3"     "spde"        
## [31] "spde2"        "spde3"        "iid1d"        "iid2d"        "iid3d"       
## [36] "iid4d"        "iid5d"        "iidkd"        "2diid"        "z"           
## [41] "rw2d"         "rw2diid"      "slm"          "matern2d"     "dmatern"     
## [46] "copy"         "scopy"        "clinear"      "sigm"         "revsigm"     
## [51] "log1exp"      "logdist"
# List available priors
names(inla.models()$prior)
##  [1] "normal"                 "gaussian"               "laplace"               
##  [4] "linksnintercept"        "wishart1d"              "wishart2d"             
##  [7] "wishart3d"              "wishart4d"              "wishart5d"             
## [10] "loggamma"               "gamma"                  "pc.prw2.range"         
## [13] "minuslogsqrtruncnormal" "logtnormal"             "logtgaussian"          
## [16] "flat"                   "logflat"                "logiflat"              
## [19] "mvnorm"                 "pc.alphaw"              "pc.ar"                 
## [22] "dirichlet"              "none"                   "invalid"               
## [25] "betacorrelation"        "logitbeta"              "pc.prec"               
## [28] "pc.dof"                 "pc.cor0"                "pc.cor1"               
## [31] "pc.fgnh"                "pc.spde.GA"             "pc.matern"             
## [34] "pc.range"               "pc.sn"                  "pc.gamma"              
## [37] "pc.mgamma"              "pc.gammacount"          "pc.gevtail"            
## [40] "pc.egptail"             "pc"                     "ref.ar"                
## [43] "pom"                    "jeffreystdf"            "wishartkd"             
## [46] "expression:"            "table:"                 "rprior:"

The R package INLAjoint

To address the coding complexity of R-INLA for multiple outcomes, the INLAjoint package was developed. It provides a user interface tailored for survival, longitudinal and joint data analysis.

It replaces error-prone coding with an intuitive framework. It also includes post-processing functions to extract predictions, hazard ratios and survival probabilities easily.

Installation

INLAjoint is available on CRAN:

install.packages("INLAjoint")

The development version is available on GitHub:

devtools::install_github("DenisRustand/INLAjoint")

The joint() function

The primary model-fitting function is joint(). It uses a building blocks syntax.

joint(
  formSurv = NULL, formLong = NULL, dataSurv = NULL, dataLong = NULL,
  id = NULL, timeVar = NULL, family = "gaussian", link = "default",
  basRisk = "rw1", NbasRisk = 15, cutpoints = NULL, assoc = NULL,
  assocSurv = NULL, corLong = FALSE, corRE = TRUE, dataOnly = FALSE,
  longOnly = FALSE, silentMode = FALSE, reorder = TRUE, run = TRUE, 
  control = list()
)

Missing values in the outcomes are handled automatically. INLA replaces them with their posterior mean during the fitting process. Missing values in covariates are not allowed.

Important control options

The control argument allows you to tune the fitting procedure:

  • int.strategy: The numerical integration strategy for hyperparameters. Options include "auto", "ccd", "grid" and "eb". We often use "eb" (empirical Bayes) to speed up computations without degrading frequentist properties.
  • safemode: A boolean (default TRUE) that automatically reruns the model with adjusted starting values if it detects instability.
  • run: A boolean (default TRUE). If set to FALSE, the function builds the model structure but does not run the INLA algorithm. This “build only” option allows you to manually modify priors or structures before fitting.

A quick tour of INLAjoint outputs

We will detail the math behind survival, longitudinal and joint models in the upcoming sessions. For now, let’s fit a complex model just to see how INLAjoint handles syntax, outputs and plotting.

We use the pbc2 dataset. We model two longitudinal markers (SGOT and platelets) and one survival outcome (death).

library(INLA)
library(INLAjoint)
library(JMbayes2) # Contains the pbc2 data

data("pbc2.id")
data("pbc2")

We fit the model by providing lists of formulas:

IJ <- joint(formSurv = list(inla.surv(years, status2) ~ drug),
            formLong = list(SGOT ~ year * drug + (1|id),
                            platelets ~ year * drug + (1|id)),
            dataLong = pbc2, dataSurv = pbc2.id, id = "id",
            timeVar = "year", corLong = TRUE, basRisk = "weibullsurv",
            family = c("lognormal", "poisson"), assoc = c("CV", "CV"),
            control = list(int.strategy = "eb"))

The summary function

The summary function returns results grouped by outcome.

Of note, adding sdcor = TRUE displays standard deviations and correlations instead of variances and covariances. Adding hr = TRUE automatically converts survival fixed effects into hazard ratios.

summary(IJ)
## Longitudinal outcome (L1, lognormal)
##                         mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L1          4.7969 0.0392     4.7200   4.7969     4.8739
## year_L1              -0.0050 0.0038    -0.0125  -0.0050     0.0025
## drugDpenicil_L1      -0.1545 0.0553    -0.2628  -0.1545    -0.0462
## year:drugDpenicil_L1 -0.0014 0.0053    -0.0118  -0.0014     0.0090
## Res. err. (variance)  0.0942 0.0033     0.0881   0.0942     0.1009
## 
## Longitudinal outcome (L2, poisson)
##                         mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L2          5.5101 0.0322     5.4469   5.5101     5.5733
## year_L2              -0.0478 0.0009    -0.0495  -0.0478    -0.0461
## drugDpenicil_L2      -0.1014 0.0453    -0.1903  -0.1014    -0.0125
## year:drugDpenicil_L2  0.0138 0.0012     0.0114   0.0138     0.0162
## 
## Random effects variance-covariance
##                              mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L1               0.2055 0.0184     0.1711   0.2048     0.2455
## Intercept_L2               0.1599 0.0132     0.1373   0.1589     0.1894
## Intercept_L1:Intercept_L2 -0.0248 0.0110    -0.0459  -0.0248    -0.0037
## 
## Survival outcome
##                      mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Weibull (shape)_S1 1.0709 0.0852     0.9446   1.0584     1.2727
## Weibull (scale)_S1 0.0396 0.0048     0.0310   0.0393     0.0498
## drugDpenicil_S1    0.1089 0.1715    -0.2272   0.1089     0.4450
## 
## Association longitudinal - survival
##             mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## CV_L1_S1  1.3704 0.2201     0.9442   1.3680     1.8109
## CV_L2_S1 -1.1335 0.2103    -1.5484  -1.1331    -0.7203
## 
## log marginal-likelihood (integration)    log marginal-likelihood (Gaussian) 
##                             -45030.27                             -45023.55 
## 
## Deviance Information Criterion:  45454.27
## Widely applicable Bayesian information criterion:  81938.58
## Computation time: 7.64 seconds
summary(IJ, sdcor = TRUE, hr = TRUE)
## Longitudinal outcome (L1, lognormal)
##                         mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L1          4.7969 0.0392     4.7200   4.7969     4.8739
## year_L1              -0.0050 0.0038    -0.0125  -0.0050     0.0025
## drugDpenicil_L1      -0.1545 0.0553    -0.2628  -0.1545    -0.0462
## year:drugDpenicil_L1 -0.0014 0.0053    -0.0118  -0.0014     0.0090
## Res. err. (sd)        0.3069 0.0053     0.2967   0.3069     0.3176
## 
## Longitudinal outcome (L2, poisson)
##                         mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L2          5.5101 0.0322     5.4469   5.5101     5.5733
## year_L2              -0.0478 0.0009    -0.0495  -0.0478    -0.0461
## drugDpenicil_L2      -0.1014 0.0453    -0.1903  -0.1014    -0.0125
## year:drugDpenicil_L2  0.0138 0.0012     0.0114   0.0138     0.0162
## 
## Random effects standard deviation / correlation
##                              mean     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Intercept_L1               0.4531 0.0203     0.4134   0.4531     0.4927
## Intercept_L2               0.3993 0.0160     0.3699   0.3985     0.4330
## Intercept_L1:Intercept_L2 -0.1348 0.0579    -0.2465  -0.1351    -0.0231
## 
## Survival outcome
##                    exp(mean)     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## Weibull (shape)_S1    1.0709 0.0852     0.9446   1.0584     1.2727
## Weibull (scale)_S1    0.0396 0.0048     0.0310   0.0393     0.0498
## drugDpenicil_S1       1.1310 0.1921     0.7998   1.1147     1.5536
## 
## Association longitudinal - survival
##          exp(mean)     sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant
## CV_L1_S1    4.0304 0.8872     2.5874   3.9237     6.0659
## CV_L2_S1    0.3289 0.0686     0.2140   0.3219     0.4830
## 
## log marginal-likelihood (integration)    log marginal-likelihood (Gaussian) 
##                             -45030.27                             -45023.55 
## 
## Deviance Information Criterion:  45454.27
## Widely applicable Bayesian information criterion:  81938.58
## Computation time: 7.64 seconds

The plot function

The plot() function returns a list of ggplot2 objects covering all model parameters. You can add priors = TRUE to visually inspect how the data updated your prior beliefs.

# Plot fixed effects for the first longitudinal outcome
plot(IJ)$Outcomes$L1

# Plot fixed effects for the first longitudinal outcome with priors
plot(IJ, priors=TRUE)$Outcomes$L1

# Plot variance and covariance of random effects (with priors)
plot(IJ)$Covariances$L1

# Plot standard deviations and correlations of random effects
plot(IJ, sdcor=TRUE)$Covariances$L1

# Plot standard deviations and correlations of random effects with priors
plot(IJ, sdcor = TRUE, priors = TRUE)$Covariances$L1

The predict function

The predict() function turns the fitted model into a dynamic tool for patient-specific predictions. It handles the integration over random effects automatically.

Let’s create a new dataset for the first 6 patients to predict their future profiles.

NewData <- pbc2[pbc2$id %in% 1:6, c("id", "drug", "year", "SGOT", "platelets")];NewData
##    id      drug      year  SGOT platelets
## 1   1 D-penicil 0.0000000 138.0       190
## 2   1 D-penicil 0.5256817   6.2       183
## 3   2 D-penicil 0.0000000 113.5       221
## 4   2 D-penicil 0.4983025 139.5       188
## 5   2 D-penicil 0.9993429 144.2       161
## 6   2 D-penicil 2.1027270 144.2       122
## 7   2 D-penicil 4.9008871 131.8       135
## 8   2 D-penicil 5.8892783 131.8       100
## 9   2 D-penicil 6.8858833 145.7       103
## 10  2 D-penicil 7.8907020 119.0       113
## 11  2 D-penicil 8.8325485  88.0       100
## 12  3 D-penicil 0.0000000  96.1       151
## 13  3 D-penicil 0.4818749  69.8       160
## 14  3 D-penicil 0.9966050  57.4       107
## 15  3 D-penicil 2.0342788  88.4       109
## 16  4 D-penicil 0.0000000  60.6       183
## 17  4 D-penicil 0.5147300 169.0       240
## 18  4 D-penicil 1.0185084 165.9       251
## 19  4 D-penicil 1.9959479 167.4       220
## 20  4 D-penicil 3.4333589 204.6       338
## 21  4 D-penicil 4.0028474 162.8       200
## 22  4 D-penicil 4.9939766 131.8       101
## 23  5   placebo 0.0000000 113.2       136
## 24  5   placebo 0.5448472 103.9       114
## 25  5   placebo 1.0705290 117.8        99
## 26  5   placebo 2.1054649 172.1        90
## 27  5   placebo 3.0062425 170.0        82
## 28  5   placebo 3.9836820 156.0       101
## 29  6   placebo 0.0000000  93.0        NA
## 30  6   placebo 1.0349359  65.1       297
## 31  6   placebo 2.0178513  52.7       296
## 32  6   placebo 3.0527872  66.7       296
## 33  6   placebo 4.0849852  60.0       264
## 34  6   placebo 6.7161319  60.0        NA

We predict up to year 14. We apply the inverse link function to get predictions on the scale of the observed data and we assume patients were at risk of the event starting at year 1.

P <- predict(IJ, NewData, horizon = 9:14, id = "id", 
             survival = TRUE, inv.link = TRUE, Csurv = 1)

We can plot the predicted longitudinal counts for platelets:

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(P$PredL[P$PredL$Outcome == "platelets", ], aes(x = year, y = quant0.5)) + 
  facet_wrap(~id) +
  geom_line() + xlab("Time") + ylab("Platelets counts") +
  geom_line(aes(y = quant0.025), linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_line(aes(y = quant0.975), linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_point(data = NewData, aes(x = year, y = platelets)) +
  theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

We can also plot their dynamically updated survival probabilities:

ggplot(P$PredS, aes(x = year, y = Surv_quant0.5)) + 
  facet_wrap(~id) +
  geom_line() + xlab("Time") + ylab("Survival probability") +
  geom_line(aes(y = Surv_quant0.025), linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_line(aes(y = Surv_quant0.975), linetype = "dotted") +
  theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Now that we understand the software mechanics and how to extract information, we can introduce survival analysis.

Q
